content:
# Joshua Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 229-230 | Codex Vaticanus (LXX)
**Source Image:** `JOS_006_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_006_page_002.jpg`

---

**6:1** And Jericho was shutting and shut up before the sons of Israel; none going out and none coming in.

**6:2** And YHWH said to Joshua, "See, I have given into your hand Jericho, and its king, mighty men of valor.

**6:3** And you shall go around the city, all the men of war, circling the city one time. Thus you shall do for six days.

**6:4** And seven priests shall carry seven rams'-horn trumpets before the ark. And on the seventh day you shall go around the city seven times, and the priests shall blow on the trumpets.

**6:5** And it shall be, when they draw out the horn of the ram, when you hear the sound of the trumpet, all the people shall shout with a great shout, and the wall of the city shall fall beneath it, and the people shall go up, each man straight before him."

**6:6** And Joshua son of Nun called the priests and said to them, "Take up the ark of the covenant, and let seven priests carry seven rams'-horn trumpets before the ark of YHWH."

**6:7** And he said to the people, "Pass on and go around the city, and let the armed men pass on before the ark of YHWH."

**6:8** And it was, when Joshua had spoken to the people, that the seven priests carrying the seven rams'-horn trumpets before YHWH passed on and blew on the trumpets, and the ark of the covenant of YHWH was going after them.

**6:9** And the armed men were going before the priests who blew the trumpets, and the rear guard was going after the ark, going on and blowing the trumpets.

**6:10** And Joshua commanded the people, saying, "You shall not shout, and you shall not let your voice be heard, and no word shall go out from your mouth, until the day I say to you, 'Shout!' Then you shall shout."

**6:11** And the ark of YHWH went around the city, circling it one time. And they came into the camp and lodged in the camp.

**6:12** And Joshua rose early in the morning, and the priests took up the ark of YHWH.

**6:13** And the seven priests carrying the seven rams'-horn trumpets before the ark of YHWH were going on continually, and blowing on the trumpets. And the armed men were going before them, and the rear guard was going after the ark of YHWH, going on and blowing the trumpets.

**6:14** And they went around the city on the second day one time, and returned to the camp. Thus they did for six days.

**6:15** And it was on the seventh day, they rose early, at the ascending of the dawn, and they went around the city according to this manner seven times. Only on that day they went around the city seven times.

**6:16** And it was at the seventh time, the priests blew on the trumpets, and Joshua said to the people, "Shout! For YHWH has given you the city.

**6:17** And the city shall be devoted to destruction, it and all that is in it, to YHWH. Only Rahab the harlot shall live, she and all who are with her in the house, because she hid the messengers whom we sent.

**6:18** And as for you, only keep yourselves from the devoted thing, lest you devote yourselves to destruction and take from the devoted thing, and make the camp of Israel a devoted thing, and bring trouble upon it.

**6:19** And all the silver and gold, and vessels of bronze and iron, are holy to YHWH; into the treasury of YHWH they shall come."

**6:20** And the people shouted, and they blew the trumpets. And it was when the people heard the sound of the trumpet, the people shouted with a great shout, and the wall fell beneath it. And the people went up into the city, each man straight before him, and they captured the city.

**6:21** And they devoted to destruction all that was in the city, from man and unto woman, from young and unto old, and unto ox and sheep and donkey, with the edge of the sword.

**6:22** And to the two men who had spied out the land, Joshua said, "Go into the house of the woman, the harlot, and bring out from there the woman, and all who belong to her, as you swore to her."

**6:23** And the young men, the spies, went in and brought out Rahab, and her father, and her mother, and her brothers, and all who belonged to her — and all her clans they brought out. And they set them outside the camp of Israel.

**6:24** And the city they burned with fire, and all that was in it. Only the silver and the gold, and the vessels of bronze and of iron, they gave into the treasury of the house of YHWH.

**6:25** And Rahab the harlot, and the house of her father, and all who belonged to her, Joshua kept alive. And she has dwelt in the midst of Israel unto this day, because she hid the messengers whom Joshua sent to spy out Jericho.

**6:26** And Joshua swore an oath at that time, saying, "Cursed before YHWH is the man who rises up and builds this city, Jericho. With his firstborn he shall lay its foundation, and with his youngest he shall set up its gates."

**6:27** And YHWH was with Joshua, and his fame was in all the land.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Method
Each verse was compared across all three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) and checked against the manuscript images of the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 229-230, and the Codex Vaticanus (LXX) folio 166. Where all three translators agreed on a reading, that agreement was adopted. Where they diverged, the manuscript was consulted and the wisest synthesis was chosen, favoring fidelity to the Hebrew over any single translation philosophy.

### Degree of Agreement
Joshua 6 shows a notably high degree of convergence among the three translators. The narrative is straightforward and the Hebrew vocabulary, while containing several technical terms (cherem, yovel, tachteyha), is well-attested and not ambiguous. EMET and LOGOS produced nearly identical renderings for most verses, with PNEUMA diverging primarily in register and fluency rather than in substance. The disagreements that do exist are principally matters of translation philosophy — how much to smooth the Hebrew into natural English — rather than disputes about what the Hebrew means.

### Major Decisions

**1. Verse 1 — "shutting and shut up" vs. "shut itself up tight...gates were barred"**
EMET and LOGOS both preserve the Hebrew double-participle construction (sogereth u-mesuggereth) as "shutting and shut up." PNEUMA splits it into two separate English phrases: "shut itself up tight" and "its gates were barred." The Hebrew doubling is a single construction using active and passive participles of the same root, and the consensus preserves this as a unified expression. PNEUMA's rendering adds "gates" which is not in the Hebrew text of this verse. Adopted EMET/LOGOS.

**2. Verse 2 — "I have given" vs. "I have already handed over"**
All three translators recognize the prophetic perfect (nathatti). EMET and LOGOS render "I have given." PNEUMA adds "already" and paraphrases to "handed over." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: the English perfect "I have given" already conveys the completed-action sense, and "already" is an interpretive addition not present in the Hebrew. "Handed over" loses the directness of natan.

**3. Verse 2 — "mighty men of valor" structure**
EMET reads the Hebrew as listing three items: Jericho, its king, and mighty men of valor. LOGOS adds a second comma, reading "Jericho, and its king, and the mighty men of valor" — treating them as three distinct objects of the giving. PNEUMA restructures entirely: "its king, its warriors, all of it." The Hebrew is genuinely ambiguous in whether "mighty men of valor" is a third item or appositional to "its king." The consensus follows EMET's phrasing, which preserves the ambiguity without forcing a reading.

**4. Verse 3 — "going around" vs. "circling" vs. "march around"**
EMET uses "going-around," LOGOS uses "circling," PNEUMA uses "march around." The Hebrew uses forms of s-b-b (to go around, surround). "Circling" (LOGOS) is adopted for the participial form as it is both accurate and natural in English.

**5. Verse 5 — "beneath it" vs. "down in its place" vs. "collapse where it stands"**
EMET renders tachteyha literally as "beneath-it." LOGOS renders "down in its place." PNEUMA renders "collapse where it stands." The Hebrew tachteyha means literally "under her" (feminine, referring to the wall). EMET's rendering is the most literal but awkward in English. The consensus adopts "beneath it" — preserving the Hebrew preposition and the locative sense that the wall fell downward in situ.

**6. Verse 5 — "at the drawing out of the horn" vs. "when they make a long blast" vs. "when they sound the long blast"**
EMET preserves the infinitive construct literally ("at the drawing out of the horn of the ram"). LOGOS renders it smoothly ("when they make a long blast with the horn of the ram"). PNEUMA condenses ("when they sound the long blast on the ram's horn"). The consensus follows a middle path: "when they draw out the horn of the ram" — preserving the verb mashakh ("to draw, extend") which denotes the prolonged blast, while being readable.

**7. Verse 6 — "Carry" vs. "Take up" vs. "Lift"**
EMET uses "Carry," LOGOS uses "Take up," PNEUMA uses "Lift." The Hebrew is s-'-w (to carry, bear, lift). "Take up" is adopted (LOGOS) as it captures the initial action of lifting the ark and beginning to carry it, which suits the command context.

**8. Verse 10 — Word order and phrasing**
EMET preserves the Hebrew word order with the people as the fronted object: "And-the-people, Joshua commanded." LOGOS and PNEUMA both restructure to standard English word order: "Joshua commanded the people." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA here, as the fronting in Hebrew is a topic-marking device that does not require preservation in English to convey the same meaning. EMET's note about the command structure is valuable but the fronted construction in English reads as ungrammatical rather than emphatic.

**9. Verse 13 — "going, going and blowing" (infinitive absolute)**
EMET renders the infinitive absolute construction halokh ve-taqo'a as "going, going and-blowing" — doubling "going" to reflect the infinitive absolute. LOGOS renders "going on continually, and blowing." PNEUMA renders "blowing as they went." The consensus adopts LOGOS's rendering: "going on continually, and blowing" captures the continuous simultaneous action of the infinitive absolute without the awkward doubling.

**10. Verse 17 — "harlot" vs. "prostitute"**
EMET uses "harlot" for zonah. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "prostitute." The Hebrew zonah is straightforward: a woman who engages in prostitution. "Harlot" is the traditional biblical English rendering and carries the same meaning without euphemism. "Prostitute" is the modern English equivalent. The consensus adopts "harlot" (EMET) for consistency with the register of biblical English that the SOPHIA Bible maintains, and because "harlot" has a long pedigree in English Bible translation (KJV, RSV) that signals the reader is in a biblical text.

**11. Verse 17 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "under the ban"**
EMET renders cherem as "devoted-[to-destruction]" with brackets indicating implied meaning. LOGOS renders "devoted to destruction." PNEUMA renders "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction." The consensus adopts "devoted to destruction" (LOGOS) — it captures both the sacral and destructive dimensions of cherem without brackets or dashes that interrupt the reading.

**12. Verse 18 — Phrasing of the cherem warning**
All three translators preserve the wordplay on cherem. EMET uses "set the camp of Israel for-a-devoted-thing." LOGOS uses "make the camp of Israel devoted to destruction." PNEUMA uses "bring the ban down on the camp of Israel." The consensus follows LOGOS's phrasing, which maintains the cherem terminology consistently.

**13. Verse 21 — Merism structure vs. dynamic rendering**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew merism structure: "from man and unto woman, from young and unto old, and unto ox and sheep and donkey, with the edge of the sword." PNEUMA breaks the merisms into plain English: "men and women, young and old, oxen, sheep, and donkeys." The consensus preserves the merism (EMET/LOGOS). The repeated "from...and unto" pattern is a deliberate Hebrew rhetorical structure expressing totality through paired extremes, and the SOPHIA Bible preserves Hebrew structures where they are comprehensible in English.

**14. Verse 21 — "mouth of the sword" vs. "edge of the sword"**
EMET renders lefi-cherev literally as "with the mouth of the sword." LOGOS renders "with the edge of the sword." PNEUMA renders "put to the sword." The consensus adopts "with the edge of the sword" (LOGOS) — it preserves the prepositional structure while rendering the Hebrew idiom in its standard English equivalent. "Mouth of the sword" is the literal Hebrew but "edge" is the established English idiom for the same image.

**15. Verse 22 — "spying out" vs. "scouted"**
EMET uses "the-ones-spying-out the-land." LOGOS uses "who had spied out the land." PNEUMA uses "who had scouted the land." "Spied out" is adopted (LOGOS) as it preserves the Hebrew root r-g-l (to spy, reconnoiter) more precisely than "scouted."

**16. Verse 25 — "she dwelt" vs. "she has dwelt" vs. "she has lived"**
EMET uses "she-dwelt" (simple past). LOGOS uses "she has dwelt" (present perfect). PNEUMA uses "she has lived." The consensus adopts "she has dwelt" (LOGOS) — the present perfect captures the Hebrew qatal + 'ad hayyom hazzeh construction, indicating a state that began in the past and continues to the narrator's present. "Lived" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but "dwelt" preserves the specific verb yashav.

**17. Verse 26 — "adjured" vs. "swore an oath" vs. "pronounced this oath"**
EMET uses "adjured" for va-yashba'. LOGOS uses "swore an oath." PNEUMA uses "pronounced this oath." The Hebrew Hiphil of sh-b-' means "to cause to swear, to put under oath, to adjure." "Swore an oath" (LOGOS) is adopted as the most natural rendering of the Hiphil causative in this context.

**18. Verse 26 — "doors" vs. "gates"**
EMET uses "doors" for delatheyha. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "gates." The Hebrew delatot can mean doors or gates. In the context of a city, "gates" is the more appropriate English rendering, as city gates are the structural element in view. Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**19. Verse 26 — "at the cost of" rendering**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the ambiguous Hebrew preposition bet: "with his firstborn...with his youngest." PNEUMA makes the meaning explicit: "at the cost of his firstborn...at the cost of his youngest." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the preposition "with" and allowing the reader to encounter the ambiguity that the Hebrew carries. The meaning "at the cost of" is the most likely sense and is confirmed by the fulfillment narrative in 1 Kings 16:34, but the SOPHIA Bible preserves textual ambiguity rather than resolving it.

**20. Verse 27 — "fame was in all the land" vs. "fame spread through the whole land"**
EMET and LOGOS render "his fame was in all the land." PNEUMA renders "his fame spread through the whole land." The Hebrew reads shom'o ("his report/fame") hayah ("was") bekhol-ha'arets ("in all the land"). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS — the Hebrew uses the simple stative "was," not a verb of spreading or motion.

### Manuscript Verification
Both folio pages (229-230) of the Leningrad Codex were examined. The manuscript is written in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The text is legible and consistent with all three translators' readings. The Codex Vaticanus folio 166 was also examined; the Greek text broadly corresponds to the MT with the expected variants noted by all three translators (the LXX's condensed rendering of sogereth u-mesuggereth, the absence of the clarifying note in v. 15, and the use of anathema for cherem). No discrepancies between the manuscript images and the translators' reports were identified.

---

## Disagreement Log

### 6:1 — "shutting and shut up" vs. "shut itself up tight...gates were barred"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "shutting and-shut-up from-before the sons of Israel" | Preserves Hebrew double participle construction literally |
| LOGOS | "shutting and shut up before the sons of Israel" | Same construction, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "shut itself up tight...its gates were barred" | Splits doubling into two English phrases; adds "gates" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering. The Hebrew sogereth u-mesuggereth is a single paired construction using active and passive participles of the same root, and splitting it into two separate clauses loses this unity. PNEUMA's addition of "gates" imports a word not present in the Hebrew of this verse (though gates are implied). The consensus preserves the doubling as "shutting and shut up."

---

### 6:2 — "I have given" vs. "I have already handed over"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-have-given into-your-hand Jericho and-its-king, mighty-men of-valor" | Prophetic perfect preserved literally |
| LOGOS | "I have given into your hand Jericho, and its king, and the mighty men of valor" | Same; treats three items as distinct |
| PNEUMA | "I have already handed Jericho over to you: its king, its warriors, all of it" | Adds "already"; paraphrases; restructures list |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS core rendering: "I have given into your hand Jericho, and its king, mighty men of valor." PNEUMA's "already" makes explicit what the Hebrew perfect tense communicates implicitly — a defensible choice for a dynamic translation, but the consensus text lets the verb tense carry its own weight. "Handed over" loses the directness of Hebrew natan ("to give").

---

### 6:3 — "going around" vs. "circling" vs. "march around"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going-around the-city one time" | Literal participial form |
| LOGOS | "circling the city one time" | English participial equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "March around the city...one circuit a day" | Dynamic; adds "circuit a day" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "circling the city one time" (LOGOS). "Going around" is literal but stilted. "March around" imports military connotation that the Hebrew s-b-b does not carry. PNEUMA's "one circuit a day" adds interpretive material ("a day") not in the Hebrew.

---

### 6:5 — "beneath it" vs. "down in its place" vs. "collapse where it stands"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-wall of-the-city shall-fall beneath-it" | Literal rendering of tachteyha |
| LOGOS | "the wall of the city shall fall down in its place" | Interpretive paraphrase of tachteyha |
| PNEUMA | "The city wall will collapse where it stands" | Dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the wall of the city shall fall beneath it" (EMET). The Hebrew tachteyha means "under her" — the wall collapses downward in its own location. "Beneath it" is the most literal rendering. LOGOS's "down in its place" and PNEUMA's "where it stands" are interpretively correct but paraphrastic.

---

### 6:5 — "at the drawing out" vs. "when they make a long blast" vs. "when they sound the long blast"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "at-[the]-drawing-out of-[the]-horn of-the-ram" | Preserves infinitive construct of mashakh |
| LOGOS | "when they make a long blast with the horn of the ram" | Renders the action described by mashakh |
| PNEUMA | "when they sound the long blast on the ram's horn" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a synthesis: "when they draw out the horn of the ram." This preserves the verb mashakh ("to draw, extend") which denotes the prolonged blast — the teki'ah gedolah — while reading naturally in English.

---

### 6:6 — "Carry" vs. "Take up" vs. "Lift"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Carry the ark of the covenant" | Hebrew s-'-w as ongoing action |
| LOGOS | "Take up the ark of the covenant" | Inchoative sense — begin carrying |
| PNEUMA | "Lift the ark of the covenant" | Simple action verb |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Take up" (LOGOS). The command initiates the action of bearing the ark; "take up" captures the inception of carrying.

---

### 6:7 — "the armed one" vs. "the armed men" vs. "the armed men"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-armed-one shall-pass-over" | Preserves Hebrew singular (collective) |
| LOGOS | "let the armed men pass on" | Plural; English collective |
| PNEUMA | "The armed men will go ahead" | Dynamic plural |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "let the armed men pass on" (LOGOS). The Hebrew uses a singular participle (ha-mechallets) as a collective, but English renders collectives as plurals. The jussive "let...pass on" preserves the command form.

---

### 6:10 — Word order and phrasing of command

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-people, Joshua commanded, saying..." | Preserves Hebrew fronted object |
| LOGOS | "And Joshua commanded the people, saying..." | Standard English word order |
| PNEUMA | "But Joshua had given the people a strict command..." | Dynamic; adds "strict"; changes tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS word order. The Hebrew fronting is a topic-marking device; in English, it reads as ungrammatical. PNEUMA's "strict" is not in the Hebrew, and the pluperfect tense ("had given") alters the narrative sequence.

---

### 6:11 — "he caused to go around" vs. "the ark went around" vs. "the ark went around"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-caused-to-go-around [the]-ark of-YHWH the-city" | Preserves Hiphil causative (Joshua as subject) |
| LOGOS | "And the ark of YHWH went around the city" | Smooths to intransitive |
| PNEUMA | "So the ark of YHWH went around the city" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Hebrew Hiphil (va-yassev) technically has Joshua as the implied subject causing the ark to go around. But LOGOS and PNEUMA correctly render the effective meaning — the ark circuited the city. The causative nuance is real but the English "he caused to go around" is unnatural. The consensus prioritizes readability here.

---

### 6:13 — Infinitive absolute rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going, going and-blowing on-the-trumpets" | Doubles "going" for infinitive absolute |
| LOGOS | "going on continually, and blowing on the trumpets" | "Continually" captures ongoing aspect |
| PNEUMA | "blowing as they went...always marching, always blowing" | Dynamic repetition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The infinitive absolute halokh ve-taqo'a expresses continuous simultaneous action. "Going on continually, and blowing" captures this without the awkward doubling of EMET or the interpretive freedom of PNEUMA.

---

### 6:15 — "at the ascending of the dawn" vs. "at the ascending of the dawn" vs. "at dawn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as-[the]-going-up of-the-dawn" | Literal rendering of ka'alot hashachar |
| LOGOS | "at the ascending of the dawn" | Preserves the verbal noun |
| PNEUMA | "at dawn" | Condensed |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew ka'alot hashachar ("at the going up / ascending of the dawn") is a vivid temporal expression. PNEUMA's "at dawn" loses the imagery of dawn as something that rises. The consensus preserves the poetic quality.

---

### 6:17 — "harlot" vs. "prostitute"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-harlot" | Traditional biblical English for zonah |
| LOGOS | "the prostitute" | Modern English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the prostitute" | Same |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "harlot" (EMET). Both renderings are accurate for zonah. The SOPHIA Bible maintains a biblical English register, and "harlot" is the established rendering in the English Bible tradition (KJV, RSV, NASB). It carries the same semantic content as "prostitute" without anachronism.

---

### 6:17 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "devoted-[to-destruction], she and-all which [is] in-her, to-YHWH" | Bracketed expansion |
| LOGOS | "devoted to destruction, it and all that is in it, to YHWH" | Clean phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction" | Double explanation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS phrasing. "Devoted to destruction" is the clearest rendering of cherem that captures both the sacral dedication and the annihilation. PNEUMA's "under the ban" is a useful gloss but adds a second term not in the Hebrew.

---

### 6:18 — Phrasing of the cherem warning

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you-set the-camp of-Israel for-a-devoted-thing, and-you-trouble it" | Literal; preserves cherem terminology |
| LOGOS | "make the camp of Israel devoted to destruction and bring trouble upon it" | Smooth; consistent cherem language |
| PNEUMA | "bring the ban down on the camp of Israel itself — and you will bring disaster on it" | Dynamic; "ban" for cherem |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Maintains the cherem vocabulary ("devoted to destruction") consistently and renders the consequence clause naturally.

---

### 6:19 — Sentence structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-all silver and-gold...are holy to-YHWH; into [the]-treasury of-YHWH they-shall-come" | Preserves Hebrew clause order |
| LOGOS | "it is holy to YHWH; it shall come into the treasury of YHWH" | Singular pronouns |
| PNEUMA | "are holy — set apart for YHWH. They go into YHWH's treasury" | Adds "set apart"; changes verb tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a synthesis following EMET's structure: "are holy to YHWH; into the treasury of YHWH they shall come." The plural subject ("silver and gold, and vessels") takes plural predication. PNEUMA's "set apart" is interpretive addition. The Hebrew clause order (into the treasury...they shall come) is preserved as it is comprehensible in English and carries emphasis.

---

### 6:20 — "beneath it" vs. "down in its place" vs. "where it stood"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-wall fell beneath-it" | Literal tachteyha |
| LOGOS | "the wall fell down in its place" | Paraphrase |
| PNEUMA | "the wall collapsed where it stood" | Dynamic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the wall fell beneath it" (EMET), consistent with the decision at verse 5. The same Hebrew expression (tachteyha) appears in both verses; the consensus renders it identically.

---

### 6:21 — "with the mouth of the sword" vs. "with the edge of the sword" vs. "put to the sword"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with-[the]-mouth of-[the]-sword" | Literal lefi-cherev |
| LOGOS | "with the edge of the sword" | Standard English idiom |
| PNEUMA | "put to the sword" | Idiomatic phrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with the edge of the sword" (LOGOS). The Hebrew lefi-cherev ("at the mouth of the sword") is an idiom. "Edge of the sword" is the established English equivalent that preserves the prepositional structure. PNEUMA's "put to the sword" loses the Hebrew syntax entirely.

---

### 6:21 — Merism preservation vs. smoothing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from-man and-unto woman, from-young and-unto old" | Full merism structure preserved |
| LOGOS | "from man and unto woman, from young and unto old" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "men and women, young and old" | Merisms smoothed to simple lists |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The repeated min...ve-'ad ("from...and unto") structure is a deliberate Hebrew rhetorical device expressing totality through extremes. It is comprehensible in English and should be preserved.

---

### 6:22 — "spying out" vs. "scouted"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ones-spying-out the-land" | Literal participle |
| LOGOS | "who had spied out the land" | Relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "who had scouted the land" | Dynamic; "scouted" for r-g-l |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who had spied out the land" (LOGOS). The Hebrew root r-g-l specifically means "to spy, reconnoiter." "Scouted" is a looser equivalent.

---

### 6:25 — "she dwelt" vs. "she has dwelt" vs. "she has lived"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she-dwelt in-[the]-midst of-Israel unto this day" | Simple past |
| LOGOS | "she has dwelt in the midst of Israel unto this day" | Present perfect |
| PNEUMA | "she has lived among Israel to this day" | Present perfect; "lived" for yashav |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "she has dwelt" (LOGOS). The present perfect captures the Hebrew qatal + 'ad hayyom hazzeh, indicating an action begun in the past that continues to the narrator's present. "Dwelt" preserves the specific verb yashav over the more generic "lived."

---

### 6:26 — "adjured" vs. "swore an oath" vs. "pronounced this oath"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "adjured [them] at-that time" | Hiphil of sh-b-' as "adjure" |
| LOGOS | "swore an oath at that time" | Hiphil rendered as oath-taking |
| PNEUMA | "pronounced this oath" | Dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "swore an oath" (LOGOS). The Hebrew Hiphil va-yashba' can mean either "caused to swear" (adjured) or "swore solemnly." In context, Joshua is the one pronouncing the oath/curse, not causing others to swear. "Swore an oath" is the natural English rendering.

---

### 6:26 — "doors" vs. "gates" for delatot

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "its-doors" | Literal delatot |
| LOGOS | "its gates" | City context = gates |
| PNEUMA | "its gates" | Same |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "its gates" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew delatot can refer to doors or gates. In the context of a city's fortifications, "gates" is the correct English referent.

---

### 6:26 — "with his firstborn" vs. "at the cost of his firstborn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with-his-firstborn he-shall-lay-its-foundation" | Preserves ambiguous preposition bet |
| LOGOS | "With his firstborn he shall lay its foundation" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "at the cost of his firstborn...at the cost of his youngest son" | Makes bet explicit as price |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The preposition bet is ambiguous — "with" preserves that ambiguity. PNEUMA's "at the cost of" is the most likely meaning and is confirmed by 1 Kings 16:34, but the consensus text preserves the Hebrew's semantic range rather than narrowing it. PNEUMA also adds "son" after "youngest," which is not in the Hebrew.

---

### 6:27 — "fame was in all the land" vs. "fame spread through the whole land"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his-fame was in-all the-land" | Literal: stative verb hayah |
| LOGOS | "his fame was in all the land" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "his fame spread through the whole land" | Dynamic; adds "spread" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew uses the stative hayah ("was"), not a verb of motion. PNEUMA's "spread" imports a dynamic that the Hebrew does not express.
